---
title: "From Association to Causation in High-Dimensional Multi-Omics"
subtitle: "Instrumental factor models for causal inference"
author: "Reading group"
date: "30 April 2026"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    css: ["default", "slides.css"]
    nature:
      ratio: "16:9"
      highlightStyle: github
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)
```

class: title-slide

# From Association to Causation in High-Dimensional Multi-Omics

## Instrumental factor models for causal inference

### Reading group, 30 April 2026

.footnote[
Paper: *Moving from Association to Causation: Instrumental factor models for causal inference in high-dimensional multi-omics data*
]

---

# Today's question

.large[
When can an omics association support a causal interpretation?
]

.callout[
The paper's answer: use suitable perturbations as instruments, then learn the low-dimensional omics variation explained by those perturbations.
]

Key object:

.eq[
$$
\text{perturbations } Z \rightarrow \text{omics } X \rightarrow \text{outcome } y
$$
]

---

# Three goals, not enemies

.mini-table[
| Goal | Question | Useful for |
|---|---|---|
| Association | Which features vary with phenotype? | Biomarkers, hypotheses |
| Prediction | Which features predict new samples? | Classification, prognosis |
| Causation | What would happen under intervention? | Mechanism, treatment design |
]

.callout[
The point is not to dismiss association or prediction. The point is to match the method to the target.
]

---

# Why microbiome and metabolomics are tricky

.mini-table[
| Layer | Rough stability | Causal implication |
|---|---|---|
| DNA / genotype | Very stable | Useful baseline, sometimes an instrument |
| RNA | Dynamic, regulated | Context-sensitive programs |
| Microbiome | Highly responsive | Cause, consequence, and transient shift can blur |
| Metabolome | Very transient | Reflects recent diet, host state, microbes, handling |
]

.callout.warn[
These layers are exciting because they respond to perturbations. They are hard because they also respond to many unmeasured perturbations.
]

---

# Mini glossary

.cols[
.panel[
**Exposure**  
Variable whose effect we want, usually omics matrix $X$.

**Outcome**  
Response $y$, such as HCC or autoantibody status.

**Causal effect**  
What would change in $y$ under an intervention on $X$.
]

.panel[
**Confounder**  
A cause of both $X$ and $y$.

**Instrument**  
A variable $Z$ that shifts $X$ but has no direct path to $y$.

**Factor**  
A low-dimensional axis summarizing coordinated variation.
]
]

---

# The confounding problem

<svg class="dag-svg" viewBox="0 0 760 260" role="img" aria-label="Confounding DAG">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow-conf" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto" markerUnits="strokeWidth">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#64748b"></path>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrow-conf-h" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto" markerUnits="strokeWidth">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#a43f3f"></path>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <line class="edge edge-h" x1="380" y1="94" x2="278" y2="153" marker-end="url(#arrow-conf-h)"></line>
  <line class="edge edge-h" x1="380" y1="94" x2="482" y2="153" marker-end="url(#arrow-conf-h)"></line>
  <line class="edge" x1="280" y1="180" x2="480" y2="180" marker-end="url(#arrow-conf)"></line>
  <rect class="node-fill node-h" x="320" y="38" width="120" height="58" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="380" y="75" text-anchor="middle">H</text>
  <rect class="node-fill node-x" x="160" y="150" width="120" height="58" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="220" y="187" text-anchor="middle">X</text>
  <rect class="node-fill" x="480" y="150" width="120" height="58" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="540" y="187" text-anchor="middle">y</text>
</svg>

.dag-note[
Hidden causes $H$ can create an association between $X$ and $y$, even if part of the association is causal.
]

.cols[
.panel[
Observed association between $X$ and $y$ may include the causal path.
]
.panel[
It may also include shared causes $H$: diet, medication, inflammation, batch, environment.
]
]

---

# Instrumental-variable intuition

.large[
We want the part of $X$ that can be explained by $Z$.
]

<svg class="dag-svg" viewBox="0 0 820 270" role="img" aria-label="Instrumental variable DAG with hidden confounding">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow-iv" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto" markerUnits="strokeWidth">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#64748b"></path>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrow-iv-z" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto" markerUnits="strokeWidth">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#3f7d20"></path>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrow-iv-h" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto" markerUnits="strokeWidth">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#a43f3f"></path>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <line class="edge edge-h" x1="430" y1="92" x2="384" y2="153" marker-end="url(#arrow-iv-h)"></line>
  <line class="edge edge-h" x1="430" y1="92" x2="590" y2="153" marker-end="url(#arrow-iv-h)"></line>
  <line class="edge edge-z" x1="170" y1="181" x2="300" y2="181" marker-end="url(#arrow-iv-z)"></line>
  <line class="edge" x1="420" y1="181" x2="560" y2="181" marker-end="url(#arrow-iv)"></line>
  <rect class="node-fill node-h" x="370" y="34" width="120" height="58" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="430" y="71" text-anchor="middle">H</text>
  <rect class="node-fill node-z" x="50" y="152" width="120" height="58" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="110" y="189" text-anchor="middle">Z</text>
  <rect class="node-fill node-x" x="300" y="152" width="120" height="58" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="360" y="189" text-anchor="middle">X</text>
  <rect class="node-fill" x="560" y="152" width="120" height="58" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="620" y="189" text-anchor="middle">y</text>
</svg>

.dag-note[
The instrument $Z$ gives us a cleaner source of variation in $X$, while $H$ still confounds raw variation between $X$ and $y$.
]

.callout[
IV methods do not trust all variation in $X$. They use the component of $X$ traceable to valid external perturbations.
]

Examples of candidate $Z$: antibiotics, diet, genetic knockouts, delivery mode, early feeding, genotype.

---

# IV assumptions

<svg class="dag-svg dag-small" viewBox="0 0 860 250" role="img" aria-label="Antibiotics instrumental variable assumptions">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow-assump" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto" markerUnits="strokeWidth">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#64748b"></path>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrow-assump-z" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto" markerUnits="strokeWidth">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#3f7d20"></path>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrow-assump-bad" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto" markerUnits="strokeWidth">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#a43f3f"></path>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <line class="edge edge-z" x1="175" y1="160" x2="330" y2="160" marker-end="url(#arrow-assump-z)"></line>
  <text class="label" x="252" y="145" text-anchor="middle">relevance</text>
  <line class="edge" x1="465" y1="160" x2="620" y2="160" marker-end="url(#arrow-assump)"></line>
  <line class="edge edge-dash" x1="170" y1="137" x2="625" y2="137" marker-end="url(#arrow-assump-bad)"></line>
  <text class="label bad-label" x="405" y="122" text-anchor="middle">exclusion rules out direct path</text>
  <line class="edge edge-h" x1="410" y1="75" x2="397" y2="132" marker-end="url(#arrow-assump-bad)"></line>
  <line class="edge edge-dash" x1="395" y1="70" x2="158" y2="132" marker-end="url(#arrow-assump-bad)"></line>
  <text class="label bad-label" x="250" y="85" text-anchor="middle">exogeneity rules this out</text>
  <line class="edge edge-h" x1="462" y1="72" x2="665" y2="133" marker-end="url(#arrow-assump-bad)"></line>
  <rect class="node-fill node-z" x="35" y="135" width="140" height="50" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="105" y="156" text-anchor="middle">antibiotics</text>
  <text x="105" y="175" text-anchor="middle">(Z)</text>
  <rect class="node-fill node-x" x="330" y="135" width="135" height="50" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="397" y="156" text-anchor="middle">microbiome</text>
  <text x="397" y="175" text-anchor="middle">(X)</text>
  <rect class="node-fill" x="620" y="135" width="140" height="50" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="690" y="156" text-anchor="middle">inflammation</text>
  <text x="690" y="175" text-anchor="middle">(y)</text>
  <rect class="node-fill node-h" x="360" y="25" width="140" height="50" rx="8"></rect>
  <text x="430" y="46" text-anchor="middle">health risk</text>
  <text x="430" y="65" text-anchor="middle">(H)</text>
</svg>

.mini-table[
| Assumption | Meaning | Example concern |
|---|---|---|
| Relevance | $Z$ changes $X$ | Do antibiotics shift the microbiome? |
| Exogeneity | $Z$ is not driven by hidden risk $H$ | Were antibiotics given because subjects were already high-risk? |
| Exclusion | $Z$ affects $y$ only through $X$ | Do antibiotics affect inflammation through non-microbiome routes? |
]

---

# Mendelian randomisation as IV

.flow[
.node[Genotype $Z$]
.arrow[->]
.node.teal[Exposure $X$]
.arrow[->]
.node[Disease $y$]
]

.cols[
.panel[
Why it works when it works:

- genotype is fixed early
- often less confounded than measured exposure
- can create natural exposure variation
]

.panel[
What can break it:

- weak variant-exposure association
- population structure
- horizontal pleiotropy
]
]

.footnote[
Horizontal pleiotropy is an exclusion-restriction problem.
]

---

# Two-stage least squares in words

.cols[
.panel[
**Stage 1**

Use $Z$ and $Q$ to explain $X$.

.eq[
$$
X \sim Z + Q
$$
]
]

.panel[
**Stage 2**

Use the instrument-explained part of $X$ to explain $y$.

.eq[
$$
y \sim \widehat{X} + Q
$$
]
]
]

.callout[
The first stage isolates variation in $X$. The second stage asks whether that variation relates to $y$.
]

---

# Variables in the paper

.mini-table[
| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| $y$ | Outcome | HCC, NASH, autoantibody status |
| $X$ | Endogenous omics features | Taxa, species, metabolites |
| $Z$ | Instruments / perturbations | Diet, antibiotics, knockouts, early-life exposures |
| $Q$ | Observed covariates | Age, site, batch, strain, weight |
| $H$ | Unobserved confounders | Diet detail, inflammation, technical effects |
]

---

# Structural equations

Outcome model:

.eq[
$$
y = X\alpha + Q\phi + \epsilon
$$
]

First stage:

.eq[
$$
X = Z\beta + Q\psi + E
$$
]

Endogeneity:

.eq[
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X, \epsilon) \neq 0
$$
]

---

# Why classical IV struggles here

The first-stage coefficient matrix is large:

.eq[
$$
\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times p}
$$
]

Problems:

- $p$ can be much larger than $n$
- $Z$ can also be multivariate
- features are correlated, sparse, compositional
- perturbations shift programs, not isolated features

---

# What is a factor?

.large[
A factor is a low-dimensional axis summarizing coordinated variation.
]

Omics examples:

- diet-associated metabolite program
- antibiotic response axis
- immune-perturbation pattern
- early-life microbiome maturation axis

.callout[
Think PCA or matrix factorisation, but ask: what variation is the factor designed to capture?
]

---

# PCA/PCR versus Factor IV

.mini-table[
| Method | Low-dimensional direction learned |
|---|---|
| PCA | High-variance directions in $X$ |
| Principal component regression | Directions in $X$ used to predict $y$ |
| Factor IV | Directions in $X$ explained by instruments $Z$ |
]

.callout[
Factor IV factors are instrument-aligned factors, not generic principal components.
]

---

# Low-rank first stage

Instead of estimating all of $\beta$, assume:

.eq[
$$
\beta = UDV^\top,
\qquad
\operatorname{rank}(\beta) = r \ll \min(d,p)
$$
]

.mini-table[
| Notation | Dimension | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $U$ | $d \times r$ | Which instruments define each factor |
| $D$ | $r \times r$ | Factor strength |
| $V$ | $p \times r$ | Which features load on each factor |
]

---

# Instrumental factors

Define:

.eq[
$$
F = ZUD
$$
]

$Z$ is $n \times d$, $U$ is $d \times r$, $D$ is $r \times r$, so $F$ is $n \times r$.

Then:

.eq[
$$
X = FV^\top + Q\psi + E
$$
]

.callout[
$F$ is the low-dimensional part of $X$ driven by the instruments.
]

---

# Outcome stage and feature effects

Factor-level outcome model:

.eq[
$$
y = F\kappa + Q\phi + \text{error}
$$
]

Map back to feature effects:

.eq[
$$
\alpha = V\kappa
$$
]

.callout[
Feature effects are reconstructed from factor loadings and factor effects. They are structured effects, not independent feature-wise estimates.
]

---

# Why factors inherit IV validity

If $Z$ is valid and:

.eq[
$$
F = ZUD
$$
]

then $F$ is a deterministic projection of the instruments.

.eq[
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(Z,\epsilon \mid Q)=0
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
\operatorname{Cov}(F,\epsilon \mid Q)=0
$$
]

.callout.warn[
The hard part is still biological: whether $Z$ was valid in the first place.
]

---

# DIABIMMUNE infant microbiome

.cols[
.panel[
**Setup**

- $X$: microbiome features
- $Z$: HLA risk, delivery mode, feeding, environment
- $y$: autoimmune phenotypes
]

.panel[
<div class="case-figure">
```{r infant-gut-figure, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics("figs/InfantGut.png")
```
</div>
]
]

Key message: early-life perturbation-aligned microbiome factors show age-specific relationships with autoimmune outcomes.

---

# Mouse HCC multi-omics

.cols[
.panel[
**Setup**

- $X$: microbiome + metabolomics
- $Z$: high-fat diet, antibiotics, immune knockouts
- $Q$: age, strain, body weight
- $y$: HCC, NASH, obesity, immune phenotypes
]

.placeholder[
Placeholder: Figures 4-5 from paper  
HCC Factor IV and molecular community network
]
]

Key message: dominant factor links diet/antibiotics, microbe-metabolite structure, bile acids, and HCC-related phenotypes.

---

# Why multi-omics may help exclusion

Suppose biology is:

.flow[
.node[$Z$]
.arrow[->]
.node.teal[Microbiome]
.arrow[->]
.node.teal[Metabolome]
.arrow[->]
.node[$y$]
]

If metabolome is omitted, part of the $Z \rightarrow y$ path sits outside $X$.

.callout[
Adding omics layers can absorb more instrument-induced mediation. It can make exclusion more plausible, but it does not prove exclusion.
]

---

# Where assumptions enter

Light critique, not a takedown:

- IV assumptions are not fully testable from observed data
- exclusion is often the hardest biological assumption
- weak instruments reduce power and stability
- factor rank and sparsity affect interpretation
- $\alpha = V\kappa$ is a structured effect model
- preprocessing choices matter, especially for compositional data

---

# What Factor IV estimates

.cols[
.panel[
**It is good for**

- perturbation-aligned omics programs
- high-dimensional $X$
- structured feature effects
- multi-omics integration
]

.panel[
**It is not**

- proof that all instruments are valid
- unrestricted feature-wise causality
- a replacement for biological judgment
- immune to preprocessing choices
]
]

---

# Come talk to us

<div class="team-grid">
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/Eva.jpg" alt="Eva">
    <div class="team-name">Eva</div>
  </div>
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/Jiadong.jpg" alt="Jiadong">
    <div class="team-name">Jiadong</div>
  </div>
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/Kshitij.png" alt="Kshitij">
    <div class="team-name">Kshitij</div>
  </div>
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/Lin.jpg" alt="Lin">
    <div class="team-name">Lin</div>
  </div>
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/Saritha.jpg" alt="Saritha">
    <div class="team-name">Saritha</div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="team-grid instrument-grid">
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/Violin.webp" alt="Violin">
    <div class="team-name">violin</div>
  </div>
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/Violin.webp" alt="Violin">
    <div class="team-name">violin</div>
  </div>
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/viola.jpg" alt="Viola">
    <div class="team-name">viola</div>
  </div>
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/cello.jpg" alt="Cello">
    <div class="team-name">cello</div>
  </div>
  <div class="team-member">
    <img src="figs/headshots/piano.jpg" alt="Piano">
    <div class="team-name">piano</div>
  </div>
</div>
